gallery.text.1: Sievert Blowtorch similar to the one my father owned. The relief valveon this one is less sophisticated but it is, otherwise, very similar. It provided some inspiration for my burner design.
gallery.text.2: Ron Weil's modification of Richard Mize's burner design. My burners were also inspired by these, and Larry Zoeller's designs, and yet, different again.
gallery.text.3: My first burner. It initially featured four columns of three holes but the burn rate was insufficient. The flame oscillated and resulted in a `pulse jet effect'. An additional four columns of holes were drilled. This burner is still in use after twelve years.
gallery.text.4: A new dual burner begins to take form.
gallery.text.6: Final pre-assembly check. A view down the spouts.
gallery.text.7: All copper parts were silver soldered with high silver content solder. The venturis, made from reducing sockets, were welded to the steel mounting. Rebored MIG tips were seated into threaded holes in manifold and silver soldered.
gallery.text.8: Final assembly ready to light up. (Run, Forsest, RUN!!)
